questions.question:
Which of the following statements is FALSE?
A. In statically typed languages, each variable in a program has a fixed type
B. In un-typed languages, values do not have any types
C. In dynamically typed languages, variables have no types
D. In all statically typed languages, each variable in a program is associated with values of only a single type during the execution of the program
questions.answer: D. In all statically typed languages, each variable in a program is associated with values of only a single type during the execution of the program
questions.to_find: Which statement about type systems (static, dynamic, untyped) is FALSE?

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of type systems. In statically typed languages types are fixed at compile time; in dynamically typed languages variables can hold different types at runtime; in untyped languages there are no type distinctions for values. Option D makes an overly strong claim that is not universally true for all statically typed languages.

keywords.explain: Types of variables are determined at compile time and do not change.
keywords.example: In C, 'int x = 5;' — x can only ever hold an integer.

keywords.explain: Variables can hold values of different types at different times during execution.
keywords.example: In Python, x = 5 then x = 'hello' is valid.

keywords.explain: There is no type system; values are treated uniformly (e.g., as bit patterns).
keywords.example: Assembly language — bytes have no inherent type.

understand.visual_alt: Three type-system categories: Static (type fixed at compile time), Dynamic (type can change at runtime), Untyped (no types at all).

given.aim: Identify which statement about typed/untyped language classifications is FALSE.

terms.meaning: Every variable has a type fixed at compile time.
terms.connects: Option A describes this correctly; Option D adds a stronger (and incorrect) constraint.

terms.meaning: Values do not carry type information.
terms.connects: Option B correctly describes untyped languages.

terms.meaning: Variables themselves have no fixed type; types are associated with values at runtime.
terms.connects: Option C correctly states that variables have no (fixed) types.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against accepted definitions of type systems. The false statement will contradict a well-known property. Option D is false because in some statically typed languages (e.g., languages with subtyping or union types) a variable may be associated with values of more than one type during execution, and more importantly the claim 'all statically typed languages' is too strong — some statically typed languages do allow a variable to hold values of a supertype which at runtime could be subtypes.

steps.title: Evaluate Options A, B, C
steps.apply:
A: TRUE — in statically typed languages each variable has a fixed declared type.
B: TRUE — in untyped languages values do not carry types (e.g., assembly treats everything as bits).
C: TRUE — in dynamically typed languages, variables themselves have no fixed types; types reside with values.
steps.note: These three are standard, correct definitions.

steps.title: Evaluate Option D
steps.apply: Option D claims: 'In ALL statically typed languages, each variable is associated with values of ONLY a single type during execution.' This is FALSE. In statically typed languages with subtyping (e.g., Java), a variable of type Animal can hold objects of type Dog or Cat at runtime — multiple runtime types. Also, union types and polymorphism allow a variable to be associated with values of more than one type during execution. The statement is too strong.
steps.note: The key word is 'all' — it is an overly broad universal claim. Subtype polymorphism is a well-known counterexample.

solution.result: D. In all statically typed languages, each variable in a program is associated with values of only a single type during the execution of the program — this is FALSE.

questions.formula_note: Type system taxonomy: static vs dynamic vs untyped typing; subtype polymorphism allows multiple runtime types for a statically declared variable.

questions.to_find: Memory size of the struct t ignoring alignment, given short=2, float=4, long=8 bytes.

understand.plain: The struct contains an array of 5 shorts and a union of a float and a long. For a union, only the largest member's size is used since all members share the same memory. For a struct, sizes of all members are added. We calculate: size of short[5] + size of union{float, long}.

keywords.explain: A C aggregate type where all members are laid out sequentially in memory; total size = sum of member sizes (ignoring alignment).
keywords.example: struct { int a; char b; } occupies 4+1=5 bytes ignoring alignment.

keywords.explain: A C type where all members share the same memory location; size = size of the largest member.
keywords.example: union { int a; char b; } occupies 4 bytes (size of int).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='340' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='200' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='110' y='34' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>short x[5]: 5*2 = 10 bytes</text><rect x='10' y='60' width='200' height='80' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>union u (8 bytes)</text><rect x='20' y='92' width='80' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>float y (4B)</text><rect x='110' y='92' width='90' height='36' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='114' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>long z (8B)</text><text x='240' y='110' class='lbl ink'>max=8</text><rect x='10' y='148' width='200' height='4' class='ok'/><text x='110' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Total = 10 + 8 = 14 bytes</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Struct layout: short x[5] occupies 10 bytes, union u takes max(float=4, long=8)=8 bytes; total=14 bytes.

given.aim: Compute the total memory occupied by the struct t ignoring alignment.

terms.meaning: Array of 5 short integers, each 2 bytes.
Union of float (4 bytes) and long (8 bytes); size = max(4, 8) = 8 bytes.
terms.example: Both y and z share the same 8-byte memory region.

terms.meaning: No padding bytes are added between members.
terms.example: Normally a struct might be padded to align members, but here we ignore that.
terms.connects: Total = simple sum of member sizes.

given.plan: Calculate size of each struct member separately, then sum them. For the union, use the size of the largest member.

steps.title: Size of short x[5]
steps.apply:
short is 2 bytes; x has 5 elements.
Size of x[5] = 5 * 2 = 10 bytes.
steps.note: Array size = element count * element size.

steps.title: Size of union u
steps.apply:
float is 4 bytes, long is 8 bytes.
Size of union u = max(4, 8) = 8 bytes.
steps.note: A union allocates enough memory for its largest member; all members overlap.

steps.title: Total struct size
steps.note: Without alignment, we simply add. With alignment, padding might be inserted.

questions.formula_note: Union size = max(member sizes); struct size (no alignment) = sum of member sizes.

questions.to_find: Values printed by procedure g() under static scoping (x) and dynamic scoping (y).

understand.plain: Under static (lexical) scoping, g() sees the 'i' from its textual enclosing scope, which is the global scope (i=10). Under dynamic scoping, g() sees the 'i' from the most recently called procedure on the call stack that declared 'i', which is f() (i=20). So static gives 10, dynamic gives 20.

keywords.explain: Variable binding is determined by the textual/lexical structure of the program at compile time. g() looks up 'i' in its enclosing lexical scope.
keywords.example: g() is defined at the global level, so it sees the global i=10.

keywords.explain: Variable binding is determined by the call stack at runtime. g() looks up 'i' by searching up the call stack for the most recent binding.
keywords.example: At runtime, f() called g() and f() has i=20, so g() sees i=20.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='380' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='160' height='190' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Static Scope</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='140' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='90' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>global: i = 10</text><rect x='20' y='80' width='140' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>f(): i = 20</text><rect x='20' y='120' width='140' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='90' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>g(): sees global i</text><text x='90' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>x = 10</text><rect x='200' y='10' width='170' height='190' class='bg'/><text x='285' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Dynamic Scope</text><rect x='210' y='40' width='150' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='285' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>global: i = 10</text><rect x='210' y='80' width='150' height='30' class='hi'/><text x='285' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>f(): i = 20 (caller)</text><rect x='210' y='120' width='150' height='30' class='bg'/><text x='285' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>g(): sees f()'s i</text><text x='285' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>y = 20</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Static scoping: g() resolves i from lexical/global scope -> i=10. Dynamic scoping: g() resolves i from call stack (f() called g()) -> i=20.

given.aim: Determine the value of global/local 'i' as seen by g() under static and dynamic scoping.

terms.meaning: Declared at the outermost scope; set to 10 in main().
terms.connects: Under static scoping, g() (lexically at global level) sees this i=10.

terms.meaning: Local variable in procedure f(), initialized to 20.
terms.connects: Under dynamic scoping, this i=20 is on the call stack when g() executes.

terms.meaning: main() calls f(), f() calls g(); g() prints i.
terms.connects: The call chain determines which binding of 'i' is visible under dynamic scoping.

given.plan: For static scoping: g() is defined at the global level, so it references the global i=10. For dynamic scoping: trace the runtime call stack — g() was called by f(), and f() has a local i=20, which is the most recent binding of i on the stack.

steps.title: Static scoping: find i visible to g()
steps.apply:
Under static (lexical) scoping, a variable reference is resolved in the lexically enclosing scope at the point where the procedure is DEFINED.
g() is defined at the top level (global scope).
The only 'i' visible in g()'s lexical scope is the global i.
In main(), i = 10 is set.
Therefore, x = 10.
steps.note: Static scoping ignores who called g(); it only looks at where g() was written in the source code.

steps.title: Dynamic scoping: find i visible to g()
steps.apply:
Under dynamic scoping, variable resolution follows the runtime call stack.
Call stack when g() executes: main() -> f() -> g()
Searching upward on the stack for 'i':
- g() itself: no local i
- f(): has local i = 20  <-- found first!
So the most recent binding of 'i' is f()'s local i = 20.
Therefore, y = 20.
steps.note: Dynamic scoping uses the call chain, not the lexical structure. f() is the immediate caller of g() and has its own 'i'.

steps.title: Compare results
steps.apply:
x (static scoping) = 10
y (dynamic scoping) = 20
Matches option A: x = 10, y = 20.
steps.note: This is the classic example distinguishing static from dynamic scoping.

questions.formula_note: Static scoping resolves variables by lexical nesting (compile-time); dynamic scoping resolves by runtime call stack (most recent active binding).
